summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Expert Evidence – Where the husband sought leave pursuant to r 7.08(2)(b) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) to adduce evidence from another expert witness in relation to the valuation of the former matrimonial home – Where a Single Expert has been appointed by consent and provided a valuation report in relation to the former matrimonial home –Where the wife opposed the application for an additional expert witness – Where the Court was satisfied that an additional expert witness is necessary to ensure that sufficient evidence as to the value of the former matrimonial home is available at trial.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

The matter comes before me as a result of an Application in a Proceeding filed on 26 February 2026 by the Applicant Husband (“husband”) (“husband’s application”), in which he seeks orders in relation to the valuation of the former matrimonial home at Suburb D (“the property”). The husband’s application is supported by his affidavits of 9 March 2026 and 23 February 2026, and an affidavit of property valuer, Mr E filed 26 February 2026 (“Report of Mr [E]”). The Court has had regard to all of those documents, as well as an Outline of Case Document filed on 13 March 2026 by the husband.
The husband was represented by his counsel, Mr Morley. The Respondent Wife (“wife”), who was representing herself, filed a Response to an Application in a Proceeding on 11 March 2026, in which she, for all practical purposes, opposes the proposal of the husband, which is, in effect, to obtain the Court’s leave to adduce further expert evidence. This response also deals with an issue relating to orders made by consent on 2 October 2025. I have made those orders pending further order.
The husband’s application is in the context of substantive property proceedings before the Court, which will be heard on 28 April 2026. The practical issue that arises is whether the valuation of the Single Expert in this case, Mr Scott McNeilly (“Single Expert”), adequately reflects what is contended to be the true nature of the property. That is to say, that it is a waterfront property, in accordance with the Report of Mr E, as opposed to a waterfront reserve property, as contended in the Report of the Single Expert.
The applicable law is contained at r 7.08 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021(Cth). In this particular matter, the husband relied on r 7.08(2)(b), that is to say that the Court should allow a party to tender a report or adduce evidence from another expert, namely Mr E, on the same issue as dealt with by the Single Expert, if it is satisfied that another expert witness knows of matters not known to the Single Expert witness, that may be necessary for determining the issue.
In short, the Court is satisfied that r 7.08(2)(b) is met on the material before the Court. I think, much to her credit, after hearing the submissions and reviewing the evidence, even the wife, conceded that this was probably a matter for the Court to determine, having regard to the expert evidence. Indeed, this issue is one for the expert evidence. One of the orders I have made in granting leave to Mr E, is that a conference takes place as soon as possible, so that the experts can metaphorically thrash out the issues and whether that results in a different valuation. The issue is important because the last thing that the Court wants is that we get to a final hearing, and there is insufficient evidence before the Court in order to make a determination of the value of the property, with the undesired consequence of possibly ordering a sale. I think the order compelling the valuers to now consult is going to be a significant step that will mitigate, if not completely, eliminate that risk. I am satisfied, in the circumstances, that it is appropriate to make the orders that I have made. I am going to stand this matter over to the hearing date on 28 April 2026.
